coredata.dc:title: Bovine serum albumin-derived poly-l-glutamic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dots embedded UiO-66-NH2 MOFs as a fluorescence ‘On-Off-On’ magic gate for para-aminohippuric acid sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Nangare, Sopan
Patil, Sairendhri
Patil, Ashwini
Deshmukh, Prashant
Patil, Pravin
coredata.dc:description: Evaluating para-aminohippuric acid (PAH) is emerging as a promising biomarker for the diagnostics of renal disease and other kidney-related illnesses. The present study aims to develop novel bovine serum albumin-derived poly-l-glutamic acid (PLGA) functionalized graphene quantum dots (PLGA-fGQDs) embedded in UiO-66-NH2 metal–organic frameworks (PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs) for monitoring of PAH. Initially, GQDs were achieved from bovine serum albumin (green precursor) via the single-step hydrothermal method. Here, functionalization with PLGA offers a tremendous increment in optical properties of GQDs. Then, highly luminescent UiO-66-NH2 MOFs were achieved using zirconium tetrachloride (ZrCl4) and 2-Aminoterephthalic acid (2-ATA) as a metal ion source and organic linker. Here, surface modification of GQDs with PLGA offered high quantum yield (QY), and responsiveness. Also, luminous UiO-66-NH2 MOFs afford a wide surface area for decorating of PLGA-fGQDs. The addition of gallium ions (Ga3+) into the probe solution resulted in fluorescence quenching (Turn-Off) whereas the incorporation of PAH resulted in fluorescence recovery (Turn-On). It is because of interaction with carboxylic functionality of PAH to Ga3+ followed by Ga-PAH complex formation. Herein, the wide concentration range and lowest limit of detection (LOD) were found to be 10 ng/mL to 900 ng/mL and 15.88 ng/mL, respectively. The specificity and real-time analysis in artificial urine validated the real-time adoption of a sensor for PAH detection. As well, it demonstrated good intraday/interday precision, stability analysis, and repeatability. In near future, the bundled illuminating PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe will be an attractive preference for tracking PAH in clinical specimens.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for sensing PAH using PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe.

Evaluating para-aminohippuric acid (PAH) is emerging as a promising biomarker for the diagnostics of renal disease and other kidney-related illnesses. The present study aims to develop novel bovine serum albumin-derived poly-l-glutamic acid (PLGA) functionalized graphene quantum dots (PLGA-fGQDs) embedded in UiO-66-NH2 metal–organic frameworks (PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs) for monitoring of PAH. Initially, GQDs were achieved from bovine serum albumin (green precursor) via the single-step hydrothermal method. Here, functionalization with PLGA offers a tremendous increment in optical properties of GQDs. Then, highly luminescent UiO-66-NH2 MOFs were achieved using zirconium tetrachloride (ZrCl4) and 2-Aminoterephthalic acid (2-ATA) as a metal ion source and organic linker. Here, surface modification of GQDs with PLGA offered high quantum yield (QY), and responsiveness. Also, luminous UiO-66-NH2 MOFs afford a wide surface area for decorating of PLGA-fGQDs. The addition of gallium ions (Ga3+) into the probe solution resulted in fluorescence quenching (Turn-Off) whereas the incorporation of PAH resulted in fluorescence recovery (Turn-On). It is because of interaction with carboxylic functionality of PAH to Ga3+ followed by Ga-PAH complex formation. Herein, the wide concentration range and lowest limit of detection (LOD) were found to be 10 ng/mL to 900 ng/mL and 15.88 ng/mL, respectively. The specificity and real-time analysis in artificial urine validated the real-time adoption of a sensor for PAH detection. As well, it demonstrated good intraday/interday precision, stability analysis, and repeatability. In near future, the bundled illuminating PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe will be an attractive preference for tracking PAH in clinical specimens.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Renal diseases have already been considered a major public health concern around the globe. In this shade, the scientific community constantly committed to the advancement of screening methods [1]. In this ray, para-amino hippuric acid (PAH, 4-amino derivative of hippuric acid) is utilized in the assessment of renal plasma flow (RPF) as a diagnostic agent [2]. Hence, PAH is a valuable agent for accurately measuring effective renal plasma flow (ERPF) in clinical and laboratory research to evaluate renal functioning [3,4]. Basically, PAH is an amide derivative of glycine and para-aminobenzoic acid. It doesn’t naturally occur in humans. As a result, it must be injected via intravenous (IV) prior to diagnosis. As an outcome, at low plasma concentrations (1 mg to 2 mg/100 mL), the kidneys can remove 90 % of aminohippurate from the renal circulating blood in a single circulation. As a function, PAH can be exploited to examine renal function as an essential indicator [5]. The renal extraction ratio of PAH in a normal individual is between 0.92 and 1.65 mL/min/kg [6]. Traditionally acknowledged indications of renal dysfunction encompass high uric acid levels and an imbalance in PAH levels [7]. In this regard, numerous analytical techniques, such as HPLC with UV detection [6], colorimetric detection [8], tandem mass spectrometry [9], and electrochemical detection [10], have been proposed for PAH detection. Such conventionally stated techniques have many drawbacks, comprising a time-consuming method, a large sample size, expensive demand, poor responsiveness, inadequate selectivity, and so on. As a corollary, there is a desire to build sophisticated types of sensors exploiting recently disclosed nanomaterials that can circumvent the shortcomings of the approaches outlined above.

As per the literature, the scientific community has dedicated considerable emphasis to nanomaterial-based recognition in an attempt to strengthen advanced biological applications such as sensor construction [11]. Carbon-based fluorescence sensors have been routinely exploited for monitoring particular analytes owing to their multiple strengths, which encompass quick recognition, cheapness, convenience, high sensitivity, and specificity [12,13]. Graphene derivatives are currently being frequently covered for the construction of multiple varieties of sensors for a broader range of functions. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are the newest modern zero-dimensional (0 D) member of carbon nanomaterials [14]. They are made up of single to several layers of graphene sheets with lateral dimensions of less than 100 nm [15]. Notably, it offers numerous appealing traits, including surface tunable photoluminescence [16], high water biocompatibility, high photostability, great dispersibility [17], low toxicity, [18], and multi-colored emission capabilities [19]. The use of green precursors for the synthesis of GQDs is revealing a new era in sensor development [20,21]. Since its inception, the utilization of bovine serum albumin for biomedical applications is gaining much awareness. Principally, bovine serum albumin is a globular protein obtained from cows. Moreover, it is composed of a large amount of amino- and carboxy functional groups [22]. As well, bovine serum albumin is an easily available, less expensive, and biocompatible protein [23]. Hence, bovine serum albumin can be preferred as a green precursor for the synthesis of GQDs for sensing applications.

Regardless, there is an urgent desire to strengthen the surface of GQDs by utilizing appropriate approaches. It can focus on improving the entire effectiveness of bare GQDs. Consequently, there is an urge to tune the top of GQDs using innovative tactics including doping with different heteroatoms. As per data, nitrogen-doped GQDs [24], sulphur-doped GQDs [25], sulfur and nitrogen dual-doped GQDs [26], boron-doped GQDs [27], etc have been disclosed for sensing purposes. Notwithstanding this strategy, amino-functionalized GQDs displayed increased photoluminescence emission. Principally, it may be because of the superior electron-withdrawing capacity of amine functionality than the hydroxyl group. As a result, it increases the photoluminescence yield of naked GQDs. As per the literature, functionalized GQDs have disclosed high intensity with boosted specificity. Here, polyethyleneimine [28], poly (aspartic) acid [29], l-cysteine [30], amino-[30], dopamine [31], etc. have been disclosed as functionalizing agents for GQDs. Since of its particular and adaptable capabilities, PLGA has been extensively applied for biological purposes. As far as we are aware, PLGA-functionalized GQDs for biosensors have not been documented. As a reason, we attempted to arrange poly-l-glutamic acid (PLGA) as a functionalizing agent for GQDs that can offer high QY than naked GQDs.

The application of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) has been extensively reported for biosensing applications. Research participants' curiosity about UiO-66-NH2 MOFs (UiO-66: Universitetet-i-Oslo) has been awakened since they have several potential applications in catalysis [32,33], chemical sensing [34,35], separation processes, absorption [36], and drug delivery [37]. In addition, UiO-66-NH2 MOFs can furnish good optical and electrical qualities [38]. Interestingly, it can be a direct consequence of their desirable chemical and physical characteristics [39]. Herein, MOFs are constituted of metal ions and organic ligands. They are acquiring acceptance due to their distinct framework topology. It offers a lot of surface area and a permanent porosity characteristic [40,41], and an adjustable pore structure with high loading capacity [38]. To date, UiO-66-NH2 MOFs have previously been utilized to detect metal ions [42], nitrite [43], and other biomolecules. It is conceivable that this is because of their surface amine functional group, which enables better sensitivity and selectivity for the intended indicator. Despite these ground-breaking benefits, MOFs have significant downsides. Most notably, it includes the collapse of MOF structures and the contraction of pores that limits their stability and sensitivity [44]. As a response, there is a demand for supplementary nanoparticles that can help overcome these significant shortcomings while retaining the original properties of MOFs. An investigation is now being pursued on luminous switches 'On-Off' biosensors, which promised superior sensitivity and selectivity than independent biosensor components. In this view, we expect to establish the PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe for PAH monitoring.

In the present work, for the first time, we report the PLGA-functionalized GQDs (PLGA-fGQDs) decorated with UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe for the sensing of PAH. In an essence, GQDs were made from bovine serum albumin as a green precursor using a single-step hydrothermal process. After that, greenly processed GQDs were then functionalized PLGA to attain a high fluorescence intensity. Herein, the synthesis of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs was prepared via 2-Aminoterephthalic acid (2-ATA) as an organic linker and zirconium tetrachloride (ZrCl4) as a metal ion. The spectroscopic characterizations were undertaken to assured the synthesis of the nanoprobe. As well, to assess the concentration range and limit of detection for PAH, the fluorescence Turn “On-Off-on” design was preferred. Finally, the examination of PAH in spiked samples was conducted in an artificial urine sample to assured the real-time application of the proposed biosensor. In this study, the PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe manufacturing provided excellent responsiveness, selectivity, cost, high sensitivity, and rapid detection of PAH. Therefore, in the future, bovine serum albumin-derived PLGA-fGQDs embedded in UiO-66-NH2 MOFs can be preferred as a fluorescence switch “On-Off-On” magic gate for PAH sensing.

Bovine serum albumin (>98 %) and gallium (III) nitrate hydrate (Ga(NO3)3·9H2O) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich, St. Louis, USA. Poly-l-Glutamic acid (PLGA), phosphate buffer tablet (pH 6.8 ± 0.05), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from Merck specialties Pvt. Ltd. Mumbai, India. Zirconium Chloride (ZrCl4, 98 %) was purchased from Chemica-Biochemica reagents, India. para-amino hippuric acid (PAH, 99 %), and 2-Aminoterephthalic acid (2-ATA, 99 %) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, India. From Avantor Performance Materials India Ltd., Thane, India, HCl was procured. Himedia Laboratories in Mumbai, India, has provided 12 kDa dialysis bags. Sodium chloride (NaCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), dimethylformamide (DMF), glucose, urea, quinine sulfate, potassium chloride (KCl), creatinine, and albumin are accepted from Loba Chemie, Chemicals Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India. HPLC grade deionized water (DI water, 0.2 µm filtered) was purchased from Avantor Performance Materials Ltd. Thane, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs using bovine serum albumin and PLGA-functionalization

ce:section.ce:para: In this step, UiO-66-NH2 MOFs were achieved by the earlier documented process [46]. In essence, 310 mg of 2-ATA and 450 mg of zirconium tetrachloride were blended into 50 mL DMF. In this case, it consumed 60 min to stir the mixture at 300 rpm. It was then packed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel container. Following this, assembly was deposited in a scientific hot air oven at 140 °C for the next 24 h. Afterward, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 20,000 rpm to separate MOFs for 30 min. The generated solid was rinsed five times with ethanol and then with DI water. Lastly, the solids extracted were lyophilized in a laboratory freeze drier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the PLGA-fGQDs/UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: For the fabrication of nanoprobe, a solvothermal approach was preferred with slight modification. Firstly, 10 mg PLGA-fGQDs were transported to a clean flask to regulate levels up to 10 mL (1000 µg/mL) with DMF and then sonicated for 20 min at 30 °C. After that, 450 mg ZrCl4 was dispersed in 50 mL of DMF and 310 mg of 2-ATA before being sonicated for 60 min at 35 °C. After synthesis of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs, the above-prepared solution was then mixed with PLGA-fGQDs solution and subjected to sonicate for 30 min at 35 °C. After this, the resultant solution was centrifuged at 30 °C for 30 min at 20000 rpm to eliminate unwanted material. Furthermore, the solution was primarily 3 times rinsed with ethanol then finally with DI water to eradicate the annoying substances. Lastly, a highly luminescent PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe was dried using a freeze dryer and was used for spectroscopical characterization and then sensing of PAH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs, PLGA-fGQDs, UiO-66-NH2 MOF, and PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (UV 1800 Shimadzu, Japan) and a quartz cuvette within the range of 200 nm to 800 nm were used to analyze greenly prepared GQDs, PLGA-fGQDs, UiO-66-NH2 MOF, and PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe. After that, the fluorescence of GQDs, PLGA-fGQDs, UiO-66-NH2 MOF, and PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe was measured using a Jasco fluorescence spectrophotometer (FP-8200) in the wavelength range of 200 nm to 700 nm. The exterior functionality of fabricated GQDs, PLGA-fGQDs, UiO-66-NH2 MOFs, and PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobes were analyzed via an FT-IR spectrophotometer (IR-Affinity-1, Shimadzu). Using NanoPlus3 (Micromeritics, USA), the zeta potential of GQDs, PLGA-fGQDs, and UiO-66-NH2 MOF, PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe were determined. In addition, a fluorescence study of the synthesized sample was performed using a UV cabinet (Southern Scientific Lab Instrument, Chennai, India). The Powder X-ray Diffractometer (PXRD Rigaku SmartLab SE X-ray powder diffractometer–SAS Mohali, India) was preferred for the investigation of the crystallinity nature of GQDs, PLGA-fGQDs, and UiO-66-NH2 MOF, PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe. The structural and morphological assessment of nanomaterials with the mass proportion of produced materials has been carried out using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with an energy dispersive X-ray detector (EDAX), 7610F Plus/JEOL (Hisar, Haryana). Raman analysis (Horiba Labram HR evolution with excitation lasers, 633) was performed for all samples to validate the modification in the ‘D’ and ‘G’ bands. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Jeol/JEM 2100) with a LaB6 light source at 200 kV was used for the size and morphological characterization of as-synthesized materials.

The ‘QY’ values of as-prepared GQDs and PLGA-fGQDs in DI water were intended via the following equation. As a reference material, quinine sulfate was soaked in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 0.54). Here, the ‘QY’ of prepared GQDs and PLGA-fGQDs in DI water was disclosed using the equation below.
Q Y

Herein, ‘I’ denote the observed integrated emission intensity, while ‘Y’ denotes quantum yield. The absorption intensity (UV–visible) is denoted by ‘A’, and refractive index (RI) of the solvent is 1.33. A sample and a reference are denoted by the subscript “S” and “R” respectively. To minimize re-absorption effects, a 1 cm quartz cell with a 360 nm excitation wavelength was utilized to keep absorption values among both 0.02 and 0.05. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of PLGA-fGQDs was compared with bare GQDs [47,48].

The use of a spectrofluorometer in this investigation allowed for the detection of PAH at increased pH using the JASCO FP8200 spectrofluorometer. A newly designed PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 fluorescent nanoprobe weighing 1 mg was first dissolved in 10 mL of freshly made pH 6.8 PBS (100 µg/mL) solution. Thereafter, a quartz cuvette was filled with the prepared PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe solution. Afterward, the sample was energized at 400 nm with a 5 nm slit width both for the excitation spectrum and emission spectrum at 20 °C. Then, in PBS (pH 6.8), various concentrations of PAH (10 ng/mL to 900 ng/mL) were produced to assess the sensitivity of the as-synthesized PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe for PAH sense in PBS (pH 6.8). In this step, different concentrations of Ga3+ ions (from 2 µg/mL to 24 µg/mL) were checked for complete quenching of fluorescence of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe. Finally, the optimized concentration of Ga3+ ions was added to the probe solution and kept aside for 10 min to complete the reaction at 25 °C. After that, the quenched fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe was measured using a spectrofluorometer at optimized conditions. Following that, the PAH sample (10 ng/mL) was incorporated into a Ga3+-PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs sensory system. After that, the mixture was reserved aside for 5 min to 30 min at 25 °C. Finally, the fluorescence recovery of quenched nanoprobe was measured. Similarly, other prepared PAH concentrations were checked for fluorescence recovery. As an output, PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe fluorescence intensity and PAH concentration were preferred to outline the Stern-Volmer plot. At last, the slope (m) and standard deviations (ϭ) obtained from the Stern-Volmer plot were employed to estimate the PAH detection limit (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ, LOD: 3.3*ϭ/m, LOQ:10* ϭ/m]. In addition, the intraday/interday precision, stability, and repeatability of the anticipated PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe for the recognition of PAH were documented. In short, the stability study of the designed PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe sensor was revealed at 25 °C with a concentration of 200 ng/mL. Furthermore, the repeatability of the method was validated using six PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobes. Finally, the percent relative standard deviation (% RSD) of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe was confirmed. The PAH analysis was performed in artificial urine wherein artificial urine was prepared using a formerly documented process. Here, PAH concentrations were spiked in artificial urine and then subjected to sensitivity analysis using the above-reported process [49].

In this regard, several interfering agents were chosen for the selectivity analysis of constructed fluorescent probe. In brief, 600 ng/mL concentrations of each interfering agent such as KCl, CaCl2, NaCl, urea, creatinine, glucose, albumin, etc. were prepared using phosphate buffer pH 6.8, separately. The 100 µL of prepared solutions of each interfering agent were mixed with 2 mL of a solution of Ga3+-PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs sensory system (100 µg/mL). Then, the emission spectra of each sample were measured using a spectrofluorometer after 30 min incubating the solution (excitation wavelength at 400 nm). For comparison, the 1000 ng/mL concentration of PAH was prepared and from that 100 µL was incorporated into 2 mL of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe and measured the regained fluorescence intensity of the sample. In addition, the mixture of all interfering agents with PAH was checked for amendment in the fluorescence intensity of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe in presence of several interfering agents with PAH.

In this investigation, bovine serum albumin-derived GQDs were prepared via a green approach and then functionalized with PLGA to advance the optical and electrical characteristics. Moreover, luminescent UiO-66-NH2 MOFs were fabricated using zirconium as a metal source and 2-ATA as an organic linker. Finally, the conjugation of PLGA-fGQDs and UiO-66-NH2 MOFs was accomplished and then it was subjected to different spectroscopical analyses.

Fig. 1 shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of bovine serum albumin-derived GQDs, PLGA-fGQDs, UiO-66-NH2 MOFs, and PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs. In brief, the UV–vis spectrum of the prepared bovine serum albumin-derived GQDs solution confirmed a strong absorption peak at 236 nm. As per data, it is ascribed to ‘-CC-’ to mediated π-π* transition (sp2 domains). Besides, shoulder peak at 337 nm is credited to the n-
π
* transition of the ‘-CO’ bond [50]. The UV Vis spectra of PLGA-fGQDs revealed a rising maximum absorbance at 286 nm linked to the π-π* transition of the CC bond. In addition, n-π* transition of the surface amine (–NH2), carboxyl (-CO), and hydroxyl (-OH) functional groups was obtained at 348 nm. The UV peak of PLGA-fGQDs has a red-shifted graph, which might be owing to the incorporation of the amide functional group from the reaction of amide from PLGA and surface carboxylic acid groups, confirming the functionalization process [48]. In the case of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs spectra, UiO-66-NH2 MOFs showed an absorption peak at 263 nm in the UV region because of the ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT). Besides, shoulder peak at 349 nm is linked to interaction of amino groups containing lone pairs of electrons with a benzene ring. The intense band ranging from 300 nm to 450 nm epitomizes the existence of the –NH2 group as per literature [51]. Fig. 1 illustrates a wide absorption range from 300 nm to 500 nm for the PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe, revealing the integrated absorption of PLGA-fGQDs and UiO-66-NH2 MOFs. Therefore, it assured the synthesis of GQDs, PLGA-fGQDs, and UiO-66-NH2 MOFs, and MOF-mediated PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe.

As per the reported data, pH aggressively influences the fluorescence properties of GQDs. Moreover, the changes in the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs solution can be observed under λmax 365 nm UV light [52]. In brief, 1 M NaOH solution and 1 M HCl were employed for adjusting the pH of the GQDs solution. In this, 1 mg of bovine serum albumin-derived GQDs were dispersed in 10 mL of DI water and used as a stock solution for further investigation. The customized pH of the GQDs was adjusted via a digital pH meter (Phoenix system 362, Systronics, India) using above mention dispersing agent [53]. When the pH is raised from 3 to 9, the PL intensity increases dramatically whereas, at pH 11 and 13, low fluorescence intensity was obtained (Fig. 2
-A). In addition, Fig. 2 -B depicts the link between the zeta potential and pH. As an outcome, the increment of zeta potential of GQDs was found in pH 3 to pH 7. Afterward, from pH 9 to pH 13, the zeta potential value declined as increasing basicity. Possibly, it may be because of the protonation and deprotonation of functional groups on the exterior of GQDs. It covers carboxylic groups, amides, and amines, that are responsible for the photoluminescence variation [45]. Moreover, the UV cabinet analysis of different pH-centered GQDs solutions confirmed the high fluorescence intensity of pH 9 samples over others. Interestingly, changes in fluorescence intensity can be observed via the naked eye in the UV cabinet (Fig. 2 -C). Obtaining results reveal that pH 9 displays more fluorescence intensity than other pH solutions.

Fig. 3 -A depicts the fluorescence under UV light λmax 365 nm, which confirmed the synthesis of GQDs, PLGA-fGQDS, UiO-66-NH2 MOFs, and PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe. In visible light, the produced GQDs appeared pale yellow in the UV cabinet which showed in Fig. 3 (i). Moreover, it demonstrated green fluorescence in Fig. 3(ii) under λmax 254 nm light, and bright fluorescence under UV light λmax 365 nm in Fig. 3(iii). It showed that the synthesized material had fluorescence at a longer UV wavelength which verified the synthesis of fluorescent GQDs. In the case of PLGA-fGQDs, Fig. 3(iv) displayed the pale-yellow color in visible light whereas it showed green color and intense fluorescence at λmax 254 and 365 nm, respectively as depicted in Fig. 3(v and vi). In the case of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs fluorescence, Fig. 3(vii and viii) displayed the milky color and bluish green at visible light and 254 nm, accordingly. Besides, Fig. 3(ix) confirmed strong blue fluorescence in longer UV wavelengths (365 nm), which may be because of 2-ATA as an organic linker having fluorescence properties [54]. At last, Fig. 3(x and xi) demonstrated the pale yellow and greenish color at visible light and 254 nm, respectively. Moreover, Fig. 3(xii) depicts PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe fluorescence which is higher than bare GQDs, PLGA-fGQDs, and UiO-66-NH2 MOFs. Hence, it assured the tremendous fluorescent ability of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe with minimal concentration. Therefore, it can provide a highly responsive measurement of PAH in artificial urine. Afterward, Fig. 3 B depicts PLGA-fGQDs fluorescence spectra at diverse wavelengths ranging from 390 nm to 440 nm. In this, increment in excitation wavelengths after 400 nm to 440 nm, shows decreases in fluorescent intensity. In addition, it shows a redshift after continuous increments of excitation wavelength from 390 nm to 440 nm. Possibly, it might be because of the variety in GQDs particle sizes and various orbital energy of the defective state. Different sp2-dependent localized states are produced by size variation. Fluorescence emission variations are accompanied mostly by an electronic transition from such localized states [55]. Briefly, Fig. 3 -C shown the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of greenly produced GQDs exhibit obvious maximum excitation peaks around 380 nm and emission peaks around 490 nm concisely. Fig. 3-D depicts fluorescence spectra demonstrating high fluorescence intensity over bare GQDs. Possibly, it is because of the existence of amine functionality on the exterior of PLGA-fGQDs.

In this case, zeta potential analysis was undertaken to assure the surface charge and their stability in a medium. In brief, Fig. 4 depicts the zeta potential of bovine serum albumin-derived GQDs (A), PLGA-fGQDs (B), UiO-66-NH2 MOFs (C), and PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe (D). Herein, the zeta potential of greenly synthesized GQDs was reported to be −16.90 mV. Importantly, the negative zeta value validates oxygen-based surface functionality such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxy present in bovine serum albumin-derived GQDs. The zeta potential of PLGA-fGQDs was found to be +22.89 mV. Herein, the positive zeta potential was obtained which may be because of the availability of amino groups (–NH2). In addition, it assured that the PLGA functionalized GQDs demonstrated good solubility in aquatic media than the bare GQDs [56]. As a result, the oppositely charged GQDs are predicted to be coupled with extremely positively charged PLGA via electrostatic attractions [56]. The zeta potential of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs was obtained to be +30.53 mV, which indicated superior stability in the solvent [57]. Thereafter, the zeta potential of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe shows +35.46 mV which also validates the excellent stability of the anticipated nanoprobe in an aqueous system. The zeta potential of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe was higher than PLGA-fGQDs and UiO-66-NH2 MOFs, demonstrating the fabrication of nanocomposites. Overall, zeta potential analysis confirmed the flourishing preparation of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe.

FT-IR spectroscopical analysis of bovine serum albumin-derived GQDs, PLGA-fGQDs, UiO-66-NH2 MOFs, and PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe was accomplished to validate the occurrence of the main functional groups. In brief, FT-IR of GQDs, Fig. 5
-(A) demonstrated the peak at 3304 cm−1, 1632 cm−1, and 1176 cm−1. As an output, it assures the occurrence of –OH, CO, and CO, respectively [58]. Fig. 5 -(B) illustrated the PLGA-fGQDs have an additional peak at 3352 cm−1 because of NH stretching. As well, –NH bending was obtained at 1455 cm−1 which confirms the PLGA functionalization of GQDs. Furthermore, FT-IR peak intensities 1628 cm−1, and 1150 cm−1 validate the existence of carboxylic functionality such as -CO and -CO, respectively. Fig. 5 -(C) depicts the FT-IR spectrum of prepared UiO-66-NH2 MOFs demonstrating the absorption peak at 3311 cm−1, 1641 cm−1, 1391 cm−1, and 653 cm−1. As per literature, it validates the existence of different surface functionality including -NH, CO, CN, and Zr-O2 stretching vibrations, correspondingly [59]. The case of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe in Fig. 5 -(D) showed an absorption peak at 3410 cm−1 assuring the presence of overlapping of –OH stretching and NH2 stretching. In addition, peaks at 1632 cm−1, and 653 cm−1 showed the CO stretching and Zr-O2 stretching in the nanoprobe. Herein, the peak intensities of PLGA-fGQDs and UiO-66-NH2 MOFs were decreased in the nanoprobe which may be because of the interaction of functional groups. Thus, FT-IR analysis indicates the successful modulation and fabrication of extensive luminescent nanoprobe for sensing.

Raman spectroscopy was utilized to explore the information about the construction of GQDs, MOFs, and further modulation in both. Fig. 6
A depicts the Raman spectra of bovine serum albumin-derived GQDs, PLGA-fGQDs, UiO-66-NH2 MOFs, and PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe. Importantly, it shows the ‘D’ band and ‘G’ band at different wavelengths. As per published literature, due to the existence of the carbon structure (sp3), the hexagonal lattice exhibits a wide variety of defects and disorders. This information is validated based on the existence of the ‘D’ band. The degree of ripples, edges, and overall size of the sp2 domains is depicted by the ‘G’ band. The ID/IG ratio is used to represent the hybridization of sp2/sp3 carbon atoms. Fig. 6 -(A-i) depicts Raman spectra of bovine serum albumin-derived GQDs, wherein the ‘D’ and ‘G’ bands have emerged at 1320 cm−1 and 1635 cm−1, respectively which is ascribed to defects activated mode of carbonaceous materials. In addition, the ratio of ID/IG was obtained to be 0.88, which represents the degree of disorder in graphitic carbon materials. Interestingly, when the ID/IG ratio is less than 1, it suggests that high GQDs were produced using the hydrothermal approach [60]. Besides, Fig. 6 -(A-ii) PLGA-fGQDs showed a ‘D’ band at 1430 cm−1 and a ‘G’ band at 1605 cm−1. Herein, PLGA-fGQDs Raman spectra showed a new peak at 1045 cm−1 which is the bending mode of an NH2 group present in functionalized PLGA-fGQDs. In comparison with bare GQDs, the ‘D’ band widens which might be due to the functionalization of GQDs. After that, the intensity ratio of ID/IG was obtained to be 0.91, which is less than 1 suggesting that PLGA-fGQDs are crystalline and integrated graphite structures. Fig. 6 -(A-iii) depicts the peak at 1420 cm−1 which is allocated to carboxylate stretching mode. The strong band at 1605 cm−1 in the Raman spectra of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs is ascribed to the CC bond stretching mode of the aromatic ring of the linker. Another peek at 1025 cm−1 was found because of the terephthalate ring's breathing mode, although a new peak at 1255 cm−1 can be endorsed to NH2 bending [61]. The ID/IG intensity ratio of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs was found to be 1.13 assuring the hydrophobic behavior of MOFs. Finally, Raman spectra of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe are revealed in Fig. 6 -(A-iv). It validates the occurrence of the ‘D’ band at 1435 cm−1 and ‘G’ band at 1720 cm−1. In addition, other bands of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs were obtained with slight modification in the peak area. The ratio of ID/IG of nanoprobe was found to be 0.83 wherein the reduction in ratio indicates the incorporation of PLGA-fGQDs in porous frameworks of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs. Overall, it confirmed the fabrication of novel nanoprobe that can open a new era for fluorescence sensor.

Fig. 6B-(i) depicts the PXRD pattern of the synthesized bare bovine serum albumin-derived GQDs. The diffraction peak of the GQDs is shown at 2θ = 20.08° [62] which assured the conversion of the green source to the graphitic nature of GQDs. Fig. 6B-(ii) depicts PLGA-fGQDs diffractogram wherein the 2θ range was found at various peaks at 14.24°, 16.58°, 18.16°, 19.58°, 23.83°, 26.08°, 28.83°, 31.33°, 33.57°, and 36.08°. It assured the functionalized GQDs with PLGA. Because of functionalization, there is a possibility of alteration of the surface sheet. As well, the space involving two graphitic sheets can increase and due to this, it displayed intense peaks. The diffractogram of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs is shown in Fig. 6B-(iii). It exhibited the diffraction peak at 2θ = 2°−50°. In brief, the PXRD pattern of the UiO-66-NH2 MOFs characteristic diffraction was found to be almost similar to the literature [63]. The diffractions at angles of 7.50°, 8.02°, 12.33°, 14.32°, 16.42°, 20.74°, 25.67°, 30.07°, 31.58°, 38.41°, 40.90°, 43.74°, and 47.16° were observed on account of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs [59]. In the case of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe, the PXRD pattern was recorded in the 2θ range of 2°−50°. Here, the PXRD pattern of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe exhibited various peaks at 7.74°, 13.75°, 25.75°, 35.91°, 43.57°, and 48.24° which confirms the presence of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe showed in Fig. 6B-(iv). Besides this, there are no characteristic peaks for PLGA-fGQDs which may be because of embedding into the porous structure of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs. Overall, PXRD analysis assured the fabrication of nanoprobes using PLGA-fGQDs and UiO-66-NH2 MOFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscopy-Energy dispersive X-ray analysis (SEM-EDAX)
ce:section.ce:para:
The morphology of bovine serum albumin-derived GQDs, PLGA-fGQDs, UiO-66-NH2 MOFs, and PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe was described by SEM. The elemental composition was confirmed using EDAX analysis. Fig. 7
-(A) showed SEM images that indicate bovine serum albumin-derived GQDs displayed to be coarse, irregular, and in dispersed form. Fig. 8
-(A) depicts EDAX of bovine serum albumin-derived GQDs revealing the presence of carbon (C) 57.4 wt%, oxygen (O) 39.6 wt%, and nitrogen (N) 3.0 wt%, respectively. Herein, an SEM image denotes the successful conversion of bovine serum albumin to the graphite structure whereas EDAX analysis assured the synthesis of GQDs with selective elements. Fig. 7 -(B) demonstrated the SEM images of PLGA-fGQDs wherein irregular shape with cluster on the surface was obtained. Possibly, it may be because of the surface modification of GQDs using PLGA. Fig. 8 -(B) depicts a higher amount of C, O, and N was measured to be 2.7 wt%, 68.8 wt%, and 28.5 wt% in PLGA-fGQDs as compared to bare GQDs which confirmed the synthesized functionalized GQDs with PLGA functionalizing agent. Fig. 7 -(C) depicts the surface morphology of synthesized UiO-66-NH2 MOFs. It exhibited a porous, spongy, wrinkled, and irregular morphology [64]. Possibly, it may be because of the formation of architecture between metal ions and ligands. Fig. 8 -C assured the elemental composition of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs wherein C, N, O, and zirconium (Zr) were obtained to be 46.3 wt%, 9.5 wt%, 35.5 wt%, and 8.7 wt%, respectively.

Afterward, Fig. 7 -(D), demonstrated the case of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe. It is a bit identical to UiO-66-NH2 MOFs wherein PLGA-fGQDs are placed in the porous architecture of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs. Herein, porous frameworks are further compact than bare UiO-66-NH2 MOFs, which may be due to the integration of PLGA-fGQDs into UiO-66-NH2 MOFs. Fig. 8 -(D) depicts the EDAX graph of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe wherein C, O, N, and Zr were obtained to be 44.8 wt%, 5.0 wt%, 26.2 wt%, and 24.0 wt%, respectively. In a summary, EDAX analysis verified the construction of a high-grade PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe that might be used for sensing uses.

In this study, HR-TEM was utilized to verify the real size and morphological structure of the synthesized PLGA-fGQDs, UiO-66-NH2 MOFs, and PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe. As revealed in Fig. 8 -(E), PLGA-fGQDs demonstrated a uniform size distribution from 3.60 nm to 12.10 nm in diameter and an average particle size of 7.18 nm. Besides, it discloses the nanocrystalline cores of graphitic sp2 carbon atoms. Fig. 8 -(F) depicts the HR-TEM image of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs. It showed a smooth surface crystal and an octahedron nanostructure with 58.12 nm of average particle size. Finally, Fig. 8 -(G) illustrates PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe size 60.83 nm. As well, the dark spherical shape validates the incorporation of PLFA-fGQDs into UiO-66-NH2 MOFs. Taken as a whole, HR-TEM analysis reveals the successful conjugation of PLGA-fGQDs with UiO-66-NH2 MOFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity study of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, the fabrication of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs and PLGA-fGQDs mediated nanoprobe results in the improvisation of fluorescence intensity with a minimal concentration of sensor materials. The 160 µg/mL concentration of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs was optimized initially to fabricate a luminescent nanosized probe. It was observed that the incorporation of further concentrations of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs does not demonstrate the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and concentrations of MOFs. Possibly, it may be because of the complete conjugation of both components. After that, PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe was preferred for the measurement of PAH in PBS. In brief, sensitivity analysis of the PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe was investigated by introducing different concentrations of PAH. Initially, the modulation in luminescent potential was monitored in a laboratory cabinet that validated the suppression of fluorescence of sensory materials than plain nanoprobe. The incorporation of optimized concentration of Ga3+ (20 µg/mL) into PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe resulted in the fluorescence quenching termed as fluorescence “Turn-Off”. Possibly, it may be because of coordination with the surface functionality of the nanoprobe. Moreover, Ga3+ plays the role of an efficient electron acceptor. In summary, MOFs associated linkers containing uncoordinated carboxylate groups serve as a binding site for Ga3+ ions. Herein, the insertion of Ga3+ disrupts the electronic structure of the MOFs associated linker. As a result, it may effectively block the energy transfer from the ligand to the metal (LMCT). Therefore, it results in luminescence quenching. Then, the addition of PAH resulted in the revealing of quenched fluorescence of the nanoprobe termed “Turn-On”. The concentration levels ranging from 10 ng/mL to 900 ng/mL demonstrated the depression of emission intensity shown in Fig. 9
-(A&B). As an outcome, Fig. 9 -(C) illustrated that the ΔF has a good linear correlation with PAH concentrations (10 ng/mL to 300 ng/mL). In this case, the linear regression was obtained to be y = 5.877x + 3779.4 (R2 = 0.9929). After that, further analytical parameters investigation was accomplished that includes the LOD and LOQ using standard deviation and slope. Accordingly, LOD and LOQ were found to be 15.88 ng/mL and 48.12 ng/mL, respectively. Prominently, the conjugation of the PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe is possible due to the interaction of the carboxylic functionality of PLGA-fGQDs and amine functionality of UiO-66-NH2 MOFs. Fascinatingly, the PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe exhibited high fluorescence intensity as compared to the bare GQDs, PLGA-fGQDs, and UiO-66-NH2 MOFs. Followed by the addition of PAH, fluorescence regaining was observed, which may have arisen due to static quenching between an analyte and a quenching agent. It's fascinating to note that PAH possesses carboxylic functional groups that interact with Ga3+ and forms the PAH-Ga3+ complex and finally the agglomeration (Fig. 10 A). In this step, the quenched fluorescence of the nanoprobe gets to recover. Overall, the conjugation of bare amine functionalized UiO-66 MOFs and PLGA functionalized bovine serum albumin-derived GQDs shows a significant improvement in optical characteristics. A summary of the detection of PAH using different formerly reported sensing methods is discussed in Table 1
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity study and spiked sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The literature revealed that the selectivity test analyzed with different metal ions, amino acids, and organic constituents (albumin, and creatinine) were found to be common sources of interference in biological fluids. Fig. 10 -(B) showed the specificity of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe towards PAH with existing interfering compounds. In this, the addition of PAH displayed the high fluorescence recovering ability over other interfering substances that assured the good specificity of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe. As well, the combination of all interfering substances (Sample Q) in the Ga3+-PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe shows a negligible recovery of fluorescence. As a result, a projected study confirmed that the PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe might be suitable for sensing PAH. A precision of fabricated PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe was determined wherein % RSD was observed to be 0.86 % and 1.23 %, consequently for intraday and interday. Hence, it promised the high precision of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe for sensing PAH. Moreover, stability analysis displayed the 1.08 % RSD that authenticates good stability of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe upto 6 days for sensing of PAH. In addition, the repeatability of PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs centered nanoprobe showed a 1.079 % RSD. Therefore, it confirmed the repeatability of the projected for PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs centered nanoprobe for PAH monitoring. Finally, spiked sample analysis was accomplished in artificial urine via the fabricated design of nanoprobe. As a response, it revealed 99.89 %, and 97.87 % recovery for spiked concentrations of PAH (100 ng/mL and 500 ng/mL). Herein, the determination of PAH in artificial urine samples confirmed the practical usefulness of the designed PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe.

In this present work, sensing of PAH in artificial urine was reported using novel PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs mediated fluorescence turn “On-Off-On” nanoprobe. In brief, highly fluorescent GQDs conjugate was fabricated using bovine serum albumin powder as a green precursor by a one-step hydrothermal method. As well, electrostatic interaction between the amine functionality of PLGA and the carboxylic functionality of GQDs was involved in the formation of PLGA-fGQDs. Then, the PLGA-fGQDs illustrate the potential of incorporation in the porous construction of stable UiO-66-NH2 MOFs. The spectroscopical characterizations affirmed the amalgamation of luminescence PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 from PLGA-fGQDs and UiO-66-NH2 MOFs. This resulted in sensory materials exhibiting superior fluorescent intensity to individual components whereas the addition of Ga3+ ions resulted in the fluorescence quenching while the adding up of PAH offers the recovery of quenched fluorescence. Importantly, it is because of the interaction with carboxylic functionality of PAH to Ga3+ followed by (Ga-PAH)3 complex formation. As an output, the designed nanoprobe provides a lower detection limit of upto 15.88 ng/mL, and a wide concentration range. As well, a fabricated sensory system offers good stability whereas high selectivity for PAH in the existence of several interfering substances. Despite this, spiked sample analysis in artificial urine assured the real-time practical applications of an anticipated luminescent sensory system. In conclusion, the projected PLGA-fGQDs@UiO-66-NH2 MOFs nanoprobe may lay the foundation for the quantification of PAH in a patient with kidney-related health issues. As a corollary, bovine serum albumin-derived PLGA-fGQDs encased in UiO-66-NH2 MOFs may be favored as a fluorescence switch “On-Off-On” magic gate for PAH sensing in the upcoming.

Sopan Nangare: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
